agent_output:

{

    "business_context": "Proposed reduction of delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental change to the data lifecycle of a core domain entity (DeliveryRecord) with severe regulatory, contractual, and operational consequences.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management — intersects with Compliance, Billing, Dispute Resolution, and Partner Integration bounded contexts",

      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (PoD)", "definition": "The subset of a delivery record that constitutes legal evidence a consignment was delivered to the named recipient. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and contractual SLA verification." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The business and legal rules governing how long specific categories of data must be stored, who can access them, and when they must be purged." },

      { "term": "Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)", "definition": "A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, which does NOT override legal retention obligations under Art. 17(3)(b)." },
      { "term": "Statutory Retention Period", "definition": "The minimum period a record must be kept to satisfy legal or regulatory requirements — deletion before this period expires is a compliance violation, not an optimization." }
    ]

  "business_rules": [

      "id": "BR-1",

      "invariant_justification": "Statutory requirement. Violating this exposes the company and its B2B customers to regulatory penalties, failed audits, and inability to respond to tax authority inquiries. No VP can override tax law.",

    },

      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without PoD data, the company cannot defend against delivery dispute claims, chargeback challenges, or insurance claims. Destroying evidence within the limitation period is legally indefensible.",

      "invariant_justification": "Destroying data that B2B customers depend on for their own regulatory compliance constitutes a breach of contract and causes direct regulatory harm to partners. This would be a platform integrity failure.",

    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Any data retention reduction must be preceded by a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and reviewed against all contractual SLAs with B2B partners.",

      "invariant_justification": "Process requirement that could theoretically be waived by senior leadership, but skipping it would be reckless given the regulatory landscape.",

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer requests delivery data from 4 months ago for a VAT audit by their national tax authority.", "expected_behavior": "Data must be available.     { "scenario": "A consumer disputes a delivery 5 months after it occurred, claiming non-receipt and initiating a chargeback.", "expected_behavior": "The platform must produce the Proof of Delivery (signature, timestamp, photo) to defend the chargeback.     { "scenario": "UK HMRC opens a VAT inspection covering the previous 4 tax years.", "expected_behavior": "All delivery records with VAT implications for the inspection period must be producible. A 90-day retention policy means approximately 95% of required records are destroyed." },
